title: Investment Banking Analyst
metaTitle: Investment Banking Analyst Resume: Skills & Examples
metaDescription: Land your IB analyst role with a results-driven resume. See strong bullet examples, must-have skills, and ATS keywords that hiring managers use.
h1: Investment Banking Analyst Resume Guide: What to Include & How to Stand Out
intro: Investment banking is detail-obsessed and metrics-driven—your resume needs to be too. This guide walks you through the exact skills, accomplishments, and formatting that get you past the first round at top banks.
audience: College grads and MBA students applying to analyst programs, career switchers from finance/accounting roles, and candidates prepping for summer analyst internships.

topSkills.name: Financial Modeling
topSkills.why: Building DCF, LBO, and M&A models is core to the job—banks need proof you can do this before day one.

topSkills.name: Valuation Analysis
topSkills.why: You'll spend half your time calculating enterprise value, comparable multiples, and accretion/dilution—hiring managers need evidence you own this.

topSkills.name: Excel & VBA
topSkills.why: Speed and accuracy in spreadsheet work separate analysts from the pack; advanced functions and macro automation are competitive advantages.

topSkills.name: M&A / Corporate Finance
topSkills.why: Mergers, acquisitions, and capital structure work dominate analyst responsibilities—specific deal experience signals readiness.

topSkills.name: Financial Statement Analysis
topSkills.why: Comfort reading income statements, balance sheets, and cash flows is table-stakes; deep industry knowledge wins interviews.

topSkills.name: Capital Markets & IPO Process
topSkills.why: Understanding underwriting, roadshows, and public offerings shows you grasp the full banking landscape.

topSkills.name: PowerPoint Pitch Books
topSkills.why: Analysts build client presentations daily; demonstrating design skill and storytelling ability sets you apart.

topSkills.name: Bloomberg Terminal
topSkills.why: Proficiency with Bloomberg, CapIQ, or Refinitiv shows you can pull real-time market data and comps independently.

topSkills.name: Industry Knowledge
topSkills.why: Showing deep familiarity with a specific sector (tech, healthcare, energy) proves you've done homework and can speak credibly to deals.

sampleBullets.weak: Built financial models for M&A deals and valuation analysis.
sampleBullets.strong: Modeled 5+ M&A transactions using DCF and comparable company analysis; identified valuation gaps of 15-25% vs. seller expectations, informing negotiation strategy for deals ranging $100M–$500M.
sampleBullets.lesson: Swapped generic task for specific deal size, methodology, and a tangible insight you delivered that mattered to the deal team.

sampleBullets.weak: Helped prepare client presentations and pitch books for senior bankers.
sampleBullets.strong: Designed and built 8 sell-side and buy-side pitch books (75–120 slides each) for industrials and tech M&A; streamlined data-gathering process, reducing turnaround time by 20% and enabling real-time client edits.
sampleBullets.lesson: Quantified output (8 books, slide count), named sectors, and highlighted process improvement—shows ownership and impact beyond 'helping.'

sampleBullets.weak: Conducted financial due diligence and prepared analysis summaries.
sampleBullets.strong: Executed financial due diligence on 3 platform acquisitions totaling $750M+ AUM; flagged customer concentration risk affecting 40% of EBITDA and restructured customer contract assumptions, resulting in revised fair value estimate $25M lower and de-risked acquisition post-close.
sampleBullets.lesson: Added transaction sizes, specific risk identified, and business impact—shows analytical rigor and value to the deal, not just busy work.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing responsibilities instead of deal impact
commonMistakes.fix: Always tie your work back to a specific transaction, valuation decision, or client outcome—vague process work doesn't impress banks.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting to mention deal sizes and company names (when you can)
commonMistakes.fix: If your NDA allows, always cite target company size, sector, and deal enterprise value—it grounds your experience in real context.

commonMistakes.mistake: Claiming expertise in tools you barely used
commonMistakes.fix: Be honest about depth: 'Proficient in Bloomberg and Capital IQ' signals real hands-on time, not just 'exposure.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Burying your strongest deal or modeling win deep in your bullet list
commonMistakes.fix: Lead your experience section with your most impressive transaction or model; analysts scanning your resume in 6 seconds need to see your credibility up front.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing generic industry insights instead of proving hands-on technical skill
commonMistakes.fix: Focus on *what you built and decided*, not *what you learned about the sector*—banks hire for execution, not awareness.

structureTips: Put your most relevant deals, models, or pitch books first in your experience section—recruit sources spend 30 seconds max skimming, so lead with impact.

Include a 'Technical Skills' or 'Tools' subsection listing Excel proficiency level (VBA, advanced functions), Bloomberg, modeling software, and Python/R if applicable—it's ATS gold and helps resume screeners place you correctly.
If you interned at a bank or corporate finance team, emphasize deal count, company size, and valuation methodology over miscellaneous tasks; one killer deal bullet beats five process bullets.
Keep your resume to one page if you're an undergrad or analyst-level candidate, two pages if MBA or you have >2 years of finance experience—banks value concision, and white space is a feature.
salarySignal: Entry-level investment banking analysts (undergrad) typically earn $80K–$100K base plus 50–100% bonus in 2026; MBA analysts start around $150K–$180K base plus significant bonus and signing bonus.

faq.q: Should I put my finance coursework or case competition wins on my analyst resume?
faq.a: Yes, if they're recent and relevant. List courses like 'Corporate Finance,' 'Valuation,' or 'M&A' under education, and highlight case wins (especially if judged by bankers) in an 'Awards' or 'Competitions' section. It signals you've done real work with real constraints.

faq.q: How do I talk about deals I worked on if I signed an NDA?
faq.a: Use sector and deal type instead of company name: 'Modeled $250M-plus healthcare platform acquisition' or 'Built LBO model for industrial services roll-up.' This preserves confidentiality and still signals scale and complexity.

faq.q: Is it okay to say 'Proficient in Excel' if I'm strong at formulas but haven't coded VBA?
faq.a: Be specific: 'Advanced Excel (pivot tables, VLOOKUP, scenario analysis)' shows depth without overstating. If you've never written VBA, don't claim it—banks test this, and getting caught lying sinks you.

faq.q: What if I don't have banking or corporate finance internship experience?
faq.a: Lean hard on modeling projects, case competitions, and any equity research, corporate development, or FP&A work. Show *how* you built financial analysis or supported a decision, even if the setting wasn't a bank. Add a note like 'Self-taught in financial modeling via [course or project]' if genuine.

faq.q: Should I list the number of pitch books or deals I worked on?
faq.a: Absolutely—it's a resume signal that compresses 'I've done this a lot' into a number. 'Built 10+ pitch books' or 'Worked on 6 M&A transactions' tells a screener you have real reps and confidence.
